coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of a novel microflower BiOCl composite incorporated with rosemary powder for enhanced oxygen vacancies and visible-light photocatalytic efficiency

coredata.dc:description: Incorporating metallic or nonmetallic compounds is a promising method for enhancing the oxygen vacancies (OVs), charge transport, and visible light absorption of a bismuth oxychloride (BiOCl) semiconductor. This research aims to improve the properties of BiOCl by incorporating rosemary powder (RPC). BiOCl photocatalysts with enhanced OVs were synthesized through the addition of 1–4 wt% of RPC using a one-pot solvothermal process. The addition of 3 % RPC resulted in the formation of very small particles on the BiOCl lamellar structures, which were aligned with the (002) plane of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The uniformly distributed CQDs transformed the BiOCl microstructure from a two-dimensional lamellar structure to a three-dimensional floral structure, leading to an increased surface area and pore volume. The incorporation of RPC enhanced the visible light absorption capacity of BiOCl. The carbon atoms in RPC readily attach to oxygen atoms in the Bi-O bond, disrupting the Bi-O lattice bond and forming OVs. The addition of RPC contributed to a higher number of OVs, which impacted both the energy difference between the valence and conduction bands and their locations. This resulted in better charge transfer and enhanced active species formation. The 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst had the highest efficiency, achieving a 98 % degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) and a 45 % removal of organic carbon within 48 min under visible light. This composite also had excellent stability and reusability, as its crystal structure remained unaltered even after conducting 7 cycles. The primary active species of 3 %-RPC-BOC system is the superoxide radical (·O2 -), while the hydroxyl radical (·OH) and hole (h+) operate as minor active species. Additionally, the 3 %-RPC-BOC achieved a high tetracycline (TC) removal rate of 82.1 %, which is 2.21 times higher than that of pure BiOCl.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of a novel microflower BiOCl composite incorporated with rosemary powder for enhanced oxygen vacancies and visible-light photocatalytic efficiency

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Incorporating metallic or nonmetallic compounds is a promising method for enhancing the oxygen vacancies (OVs), charge transport, and visible light absorption of a bismuth oxychloride (BiOCl) semiconductor. This research aims to improve the properties of BiOCl by incorporating rosemary powder (RPC). BiOCl photocatalysts with enhanced OVs were synthesized through the addition of 1–4 wt% of RPC using a one-pot solvothermal process. The addition of 3 % RPC resulted in the formation of very small particles on the BiOCl lamellar structures, which were aligned with the (002) plane of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The uniformly distributed CQDs transformed the BiOCl microstructure from a two-dimensional lamellar structure to a three-dimensional floral structure, leading to an increased surface area and pore volume. The incorporation of RPC enhanced the visible light absorption capacity of BiOCl. The carbon atoms in RPC readily attach to oxygen atoms in the Bi-O bond, disrupting the Bi-O lattice bond and forming OVs. The addition of RPC contributed to a higher number of OVs, which impacted both the energy difference between the valence and conduction bands and their locations. This resulted in better charge transfer and enhanced active species formation. The 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst had the highest efficiency, achieving a 98 % degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) and a 45 % removal of organic carbon within 48 min under visible light. This composite also had excellent stability and reusability, as its crystal structure remained unaltered even after conducting 7 cycles. The primary active species of 3 %-RPC-BOC system is the superoxide radical (·O2 -), while the hydroxyl radical (·OH) and hole (h+) operate as minor active species. Additionally, the 3 %-RPC-BOC achieved a high tetracycline (TC) removal rate of 82.1 %, which is 2.21 times higher than that of pure BiOCl.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The utilization of photocatalytic oxidation technology has garnered considerable attention due to its numerous advantages, including low energy consumption, high removal efficiency, excellent chemical stability, and robust redox capacity in treating both wastewater and airborne pollutants [1,2]. The effectiveness of photocatalytic degradation is dependent on the properties of the photocatalyst [3]. The ability of a photocatalyst to effectively absorb light and efficiently separate charge carriers significantly influences its performance [4]. BiOCl stands out among various semiconductor-based photocatalysts due to its unique lamellar microscopic structure and internal electric field, which facilitate the effective separation and transport of electrons and holes. Furthermore, BiOCl has a broad range of light responses, hence enhancing its ability to absorb sunlight. However, BiOCl faces challenges such as rapid carrier recombination and a limited spectrum of visible light absorption during photocatalytic degradation [5]. The comprehensive review of various strategies to improve BiOCl's photocatalytic performance includes fabricating heterojunctions, introducing defects, incorporating metallic or nonmetallic elements such as manganese [6], nickel [7,8], and bismuth [9], and modifying the morphology of BiOCl. The review indicates the wide range of applications for BiOCl-based photocatalysts, such as organic pollutant photodegradation [10], photocatalytic hydrogen production [10,11], CO2 photoreduction [12,13], and more. This extensive coverage in recent research emphasizes the need for innovative synthesis approaches for tailoring the characteristics of BiOCl-based photocatalysts for various applications.

The addition of AgIO3 in appropriate amounts to BiOCl resulted in the formation of a heterojunction. This heterojunction decreased the energy difference between the valence and conduction bands, therefore broadening the range of visible light. The photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride was much improved compared to using pure BiOCl [3]. Similarly, the addition of Mn created metal sites on the lamellae. This improved the separation of electron-hole pairs, reducing their recombination and leading to a 16 % increase in metronidazole decomposition efficiency [6]. Recent developments in BiOCl-based photocatalysts, including the incorporation of cobalt [14] and sulfur [15], enhanced the separation of charges and absorption of light while also simplifying the synthesis process for large-scale applications. The study of Ni-modified BiOBr has significantly advanced the field of CO2 photoreduction [8]. The addition of Ni to BiOBr increased the yield of CO by 4.3 times. This enhancement was achieved through improvements in the dipole moment and internal electric field, which improved the separation of electron-hole pairs. Furthermore, the beneficial combination of BiOBr with Ni-MOF in a hybrid material significantly enhanced efficiency, resulting in a 5- to 7.2-fold increase in the rate of CO evolution compared to the individual components [7]. This improvement was attributed to better CO2 adsorption and improved separation of carriers. The high recombination rate of photogenerated carriers in BiOBr restricted its effectiveness in CO₂ reduction. The issue was successfully resolved by applying a reduced Bi onto the BiOBr surface [9]. The combination of ohmic contact with the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effect produced a synergistic effect that enhanced the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The rapid degradation of fluoroquinolone antibiotics under visible light was achieved by transforming the microstructure of BiOBr from a layered arrangement to a flower-like arrangement using polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) as an additive [16]. This transformation allowed multiple reflections of visible light within the BiOBr, enhancing its light utilization efficiency. Photocatalysts can improve their efficiency in decomposing compounds by incorporating them with other functional materials, hence acquiring distinctive characteristics. The addition of multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) and CQDs to BiOCl significantly improved the separation rate of photogenerated carriers [5,17]. The MWCNTs/BiOCl composite exhibited a higher number of active sites and a greater adsorption capacity, leading to an improvement in the photocatalytic degradation efficiency. CQDs have the potential to be activated by low-energy light and emit high-energy light. It has an important impact on the widespread consumption of light. Consequently, the CQDs/BiOCl composite showed better photocatalytic activity compared to pure BiOCl in the degradation of perfluorooctanoic acid [10].

Introducing defects in a photocatalyst is a method used to improve the photocatalyst's capacity for converting light into chemical energy. Vacancies, a type of defect that can be easily formed, are commonly used to improve the efficiency of the photocatalyst. Oxygen vacancies are frequently present in materials containing oxygen. The presence of oxygen vacancies in photocatalysts has a notable impact on enriching the quantity of electrons, thus enhancing the separation and efficiency of carrier transport. Electrons and oxygen atoms perform reactions when exposed to light, leading to the formation of active superoxide radicals. These radicals play an important role in breaking down contaminants. BiOCl has a low bond energy in the Bi-O bond, allowing oxygen atoms to readily detach from the (010) plane of BiOCl when the bond between them is disrupted. This detachment leads to the formation of OVs [18], which enhance the ability to capture light-excited electrons and improve the performance of photocatalysts.

Conventional chemical reactions have been used to produce OVs in BiOCl, but these methods have challenges such as high energy consumption and safety risks. A more efficient alternative approach involves using biomass rich in radicals and carbon sources to generate oxygen vacancies during photocatalyst preparation. This approach offers benefits for overcoming the challenges caused by chemical methods. For example, the addition of panax powder to the preparation of BiOCl increased the number of oxygen vacancies, significantly enhancing its ability to degrade substances through photocatalysis [19].

The use of natural materials in catalyst synthesis is gaining more attention due to the potential benefits derived from their unique chemical compositions [10]. Rosemary, a versatile herb used in various applications, is remarkable for its wide availability, simplicity, ease of acquisition, and cost-effectiveness [20]. The resource's worldwide durability enables its widespread availability. The unique properties of rosemary make it a desirable option for incorporation into catalyst synthesis. Rosemary is known for its bioactive compounds, such as rosmarinic acid, essential oils, and flavonoids. It does include a variety of organic compounds, all of which contain carbon atoms and may be helpful in the development of effective catalysts. Furthermore, the investigation of rosemary as a raw material in the production of photocatalysts aligns with the growing trend towards sustainable and environmentally friendly approaches in the area of materials science.

This study focused on the synthesis of BiOCl incorporated with RPC (RPC-BOC) photocatalysts in order to modify microstructures and enhance OVs, which directly affect degradation efficiency. The effects of RPC contents on the characteristics and performance of the RPC-BOC composites were investigated. The RPC-BOC composites were thoroughly analyzed using various methods to examine their crystal structures, morphology, lattice fringes, charge transport efficiency, presence of oxygen vacancies, elemental composition, and surface valence states. The parameters of surface photovoltage, photocurrent, electrochemical impedance, and optical absorption were also examined. The study evaluated the performance of RPC-BOC photocatalysts in the degradation of RhB and TC, as well as the reusability and stability of the photocatalyst. The active radicals and the transfer mechanisms involved in the photocatalytic process were proposed.
The chemicals utilized in this investigation were supplied by Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd. They were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

Rosemary leaves were naturally dried in the air, then ground and sieved through a 100-mesh sieve. The ground material was then immersed in a 1 mol/L nitric acid solution and magnetically stirred for 24 h. The solid was filtered and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C. The resulting product was immersed in 1 mol/L sodium hydroxide solution and processed in the same method as the acid immersion to obtain rosemary powder, which was designated as RPC.

The RPC-BOC photocatalysts were synthesized through a solvothermal reaction. First, 2.5 mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was completely dissolved in 40 mL of glacial acetic acid. Subsequently, the desired amount of RPC was introduced to the solution and stirred for 10 h. A solution containing 2.5 mmol of KCl in 10 mL of glacial acetic acid was gradually injected into the initial solution using a microperistaltic pump over a period of 30 min. The two solutions were thoroughly mixed for 60 min. The resulting emulsion was then transferred to a 100-mL autoclave, where it conducted a reaction at 180 °C for 22 h. After the reaction was complete, the substance was cooled to room temperature, and the resulting solid was washed several times with distilled water and anhydrous ethanol. The washed solids were subsequently dried in an oven at 50 °C for 24 h. The resulting dried powder was ground and designated as x%-RPC-BOC, where x signifies the percentage of RPC added.

The performance of the RPC-BOC photocatalysts in degrading pollutants was assessed through a photocatalytic reaction of modelled pollutants. The experimental setup utilized a photocatalytic reactor equipped with a xenon lamp power set at 400 W. This study chose RhB and TC as the simulated pollutants. In the initial step, 50 mL of a 10 mg/L RhB solution was introduced into a 100 mL reaction tube along with 50 mg of the photocatalyst. The reaction started in darkness for 30 min, followed by a subsequent exposure to light for 48 min. During the reaction, the samples were taken at 12-min intervals for analysis. The collected samples were subjected to centrifugation and filtration through a 0.22-μm filter membrane to separate the photocatalyst. The residual concentration of RhB was determined using a TU-1901 UV-Vis spectrophotometer at 554 nm, while the total organic carbon (TOC) content in the solution was quantified using a Vario TOC Select organic carbon analyzer. In order to assess the effectiveness of the photocatalysts in degrading various types of organic compounds, an experiment was conducted to evaluate the degradation of TC. The initial concentration of TC was 10 mg/L. The reaction solutions were periodically corrected every 30 min during the 180-min degrading process under light. The dark reaction duration, catalyst dose, and light source intensity were consistent with the RhB degradation tests. The total organic carbon content in the solutions throughout the reaction was examined.

The photocatalyst's crystal structures were investigated using X-ray diffractometry (XRD). The microscopic morphology of the photocatalysts was examined through a scanning electron microscope (SEM). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was employed to scrutinize lattice fringing and microstructure. Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) was applied to evaluate the charge transport efficiency of the photocatalysts. Low-temperature electron spin resonance (ESR) was performed to assess the presence of oxygen vacancies in the photocatalysts. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to analyze the elemental species and valence states on the photocatalyst's surface. The surface photovoltage of the photocatalysts was investigated using surface photovoltage spectroscopy (SPS). The photocurrent (PC) and electrochemical impedance (EIS) were obtained from an electrochemical workstation. The optical absorption properties of the photocatalysts were studied by ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV-Vis DRS).

The XRD patterns of BiOCl photocatalysts with and without the addition of RPC are given in Fig. 1a. The standard spectrum of the BiOCl tetragonal crystalline structure (PDF#06-0249) was used as a reference. The XRD peak positions of all the photocatalysts were consistent with the standard spectrum, indicating the presence of a tetragonal crystalline phase. The diffraction angles at 2θ are 12.03°, 25.93°, 32.49°, 33.53°, and 46.64°, corresponding to the (001), (101), (110), (102), and (200) crystal planes of BiOCl [21,22]. A variation in peak intensity was detected in the (110) crystal plane of the photocatalysts when different amounts of RPC were used. This suggests that RPC had an impact on the growth of crystals during the solvothermal process [23]. Fig. 1b shows that the incorporation of RPC slightly shifted the diffraction angle of the (001) crystal plane from 12.03°, suggesting that RPC had an impact on the structures of the (001) crystal plane during the formation of BiOCl crystals. When carbon atoms in the RPC are incorporated into the BiOCl lattice, they may induce a slight contraction or expansion of the lattice, depending on the amount of RPC. The diffraction peaks of 1 %- to 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalysts show a shift towards lower angles, while only the 4 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst exhibits a shift towards higher angles compared to pure BiOCl. This phenomenon can be explained by Bragg's equation (nλ = 2dsinθ), where d represents the spacing between crystal planes of the material, θ is the Bragg angle, λ is the wavelength of the incident X-ray beam, and n is the degree of reflection. The decrease in angle seen in the peak shift for RPC of less than 4 % may be attributed to an increase in interlayer spacing. Conversely, the higher angle shift observed for the 4 %-RPC-BOC is a result of a reduction in interlayer spacing. This reduction may be due to the space-site resistance effect of the carbon atoms at higher RPC contents, which hindered crystal growth and decreased the interlayer spacing. In Fig. 1c, the energy spectrum of the 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst reveals the existence of four elements, including Bi, O, Cl, and C. This result confirms the incorporation of carbon from RPC in the photocatalyst.

The photocatalyst microstructure was thoroughly characterized using SEM and HRTEM techniques, with the results presented in Fig. 2. The SEM image of pure BiOCl (Fig. 2a) displays an irregular arrangement of two-dimensional flakes with different particle sizes and an uneven distribution. In contrast, the SEM image of 3 %-RPC-BOC (Fig. 2b) reveals a three-dimensional flower-like microscopic morphology. The inclusion of RPC resulted in a significant change in the BiOCl microstructure, transforming it from a disordered arrangement of stacked sheets to a uniform size with a flower-shaped structure. The latter configuration provided a larger specific surface area and pore volume, which might enhance light absorption.

The HRTEM image (Fig. 2c) shows irregularities on the layer of 3 %-RPC-BOC, indicating a defect. These defects may promote the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, leading to enhanced photocatalytic efficiency [24]. Furthermore, HRTEM detected the presence of CQDs in the 3 %-RPC-BOC. The absence of diffraction peaks in CQDs formed with 3 %-RPC-BOC might be ascribed to the low percentage of RPC additions and the lack of a crystal structure in the CQDs. In Fig. 2d, one can notice black specks with a lattice stripe width of 0.331 nm, which corresponds to the (002) plane of CQDs [5]. These specks exhibit a uniform size. Fig. 2e identified three different types of lattice stripes, each with a specific spacing of 0.331 nm, 0.344 nm, and 0.368 nm. These stripes correspond to the CQDs (002) plane, the BiOCl (110) crystal plane, and the BiOCl (200) crystal plane, respectively [5,25]. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of 3 %-RPC-BOC is depicted in Fig. 2f. The diffraction spots in SAED were observed for particular crystal facets of (110) and (200) of a single-crystalline structure of BiOCl on the zone axis of [001] [15]. It is important to highlight that the 3 %-RPC-BOC had a single-crystal structure, which aligns with the XRD results. Moreover, the EDS mappings of the 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst revealed a uniform dispersion of carbon over its surface, as shown in Fig. 2g and h.

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution profiles of the BiOCl and 3 %-RPC-BOC are illustrated in Fig. 3. According to the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) classification [26], the isotherms observed in Fig. 3a for both BiOCl and 3 %-RPC-BOC may be classified as type IV, characterized by H3 hysteresis loops. The hysteresis loop of 3 %-RPC-BOC had a larger width compared to that of BiOCl, suggesting a probable variation in the adsorption-desorption process owing to the modified pore structures. The pore volumes of BiOCl and 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalysts are 0.0053 cm3/g and 0.0225 cm3/g, respectively, with average pore diameters of 27 nm and 21 nm, as seen in Fig. 3b. These two photocatalysts are mesoporous materials. In contrast to the BiOCl, the 3 %-RPC-BOC exhibits an increased specific surface area and a larger pore volume. The enhancement was attributed to the incorporation of RPC, which transformed the morphology from flake-like to flower-like structures and introduced surface defects. The presence of these defects promoted photogenerated charge separation [27], while the increased specific surface area and pore volume provided more active sites [28]. The photocatalytic degradation tests will confirm the improved performance of the 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst.

The FT-IR spectra of all photocatalysts, as shown in Fig. 4a, exhibit a prominent peak at 524 cm−1. This peak corresponds to the stretching vibration of the Bi-O bond, which is typical of BiOCl. The peak intensity of different photocatalysts gradually decreased as the amount of RPC increased. This decrease in intensity was attributed to the formation of OVs in the photocatalysts [29]. Two additional peaks at 3435 cm−1 and 1622 cm−1 were observed, indicating the stretching vibration of water molecules or -OH groups absorbed on the surface of the photocatalyst [30]. The C-O bond vibration corresponds to the vibrational peak observed at 2320 cm−1 [31]. The C-O bond had a bond length of 0.12 nm and a bond energy of 755 KJ/mol, whereas the Bi-O bond had a bond length of 0.19 nm and a bond energy of 377 KJ/mol. The C-O bond's shorter bond length and higher bond energy compared to the Bi-O bond made the latter more susceptible to breaking [32]. Thus, the carbon in the RPC easily reacted with the oxygen atoms in the Bi-O bonds during the photocatalyst preparation process [33]. This reaction broke the Bi-O bonds in the lattice, leading to the formation of OVs [34]. Fig. 4b displays the photoluminescence spectra of all photocatalysts. A significant peak within the wavelength range of 600–660 nm was observed for each photocatalyst. The PL intensity of BiOCl was greater than that of RPC-BOC photocatalysts. The PL intensity of 3 %-RPC-BOC was the lowest. The incorporation of RPC into BiOCl caused a shift in the PL wavelength, possibly due to the fluorescence effect of RPC. A smaller PL peak was due to a decreased charge transport resistance, which promoted efficient carrier separation and transport [35].

XPS was applied to perform a more in-depth analysis of the chemical composition and valence state. The XPS spectra of the photocatalysts in Fig. 5a show that they contain Bi, O, Cl, and C elements. This confirms that carbon has been added to the surface of the BiOCl photocatalyst. This result was in accordance with the results obtained from HRTEM and EDS. The Bi 4f spectra in Fig. 5b were analyzed for all photocatalysts, indicating two separate Bi signal peaks at 158.8 eV and 164.1 eV for pure BiOCl. These peaks correspond to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 of Bi3+, respectively. The introduction of RPC changed the binding energies of Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 in the photocatalysts to 159.2 eV and 164.6 eV, respectively. These peaks confirm the presence of Bi3+ in the crystal structures [36]. The observed slight deviation in the Bi 4f peaks indicates a change in the amount of Bi3+ due to the Bi-C interaction. The different carbon forms in the 3 %-RPC-BOC can be seen in Fig. 5c, which is the result of deconvolution of the C 1s spectra. These forms were identified as CO, C-O, and C-C bonds, with bond energies of 284.8 eV, 286.3 eV, and 288.3 eV, respectively [37]. Fig. 5d detects the binding energy of the Cl 2p peak in BiOCl at 197.85 eV. Notably, the addition of RPC contents of more than 1 % consistently changed the Cl 2p XPS spectra to show higher binding energies. This shows that adding more than 1 % RPC changed the electronic environment around the Cl atoms even more. Their binding energy shifted slightly to approximately 198.05 eV. The shift was attributed to the production of OVs, which affect the bond energy and length of the Bi2O2 2+ and Cl- bonds. The observed directional shift in Bi 4f was consistent. In Fig. 5e and f, the O 1s spectra of BiOCl and 3 %-RPC-BOC show three peaks that correspond to water molecules or hydroxyl groups, oxygen atoms adsorbed on the OVs, and oxygen in the Bi-O bonds, respectively [38]. The presence of hydroxyl groups or water molecules in 3 %-RPC-BOC did not exhibit significant changes when compared to BiOCl. However, the quantity of oxygen atoms adsorbed on OVs exhibited an increase, while the quantity of Bi-O bonds showed a decrease, suggesting that RPC had an impact on the number of OVs in the photocatalyst. The carbon atoms in the RPC can chemically react with the oxygen atoms in the Bi-O bonds, resulting in the destruction of the Bi-O bonds. This caused differences in the binding energy positions of the Bi 4f and the intensities of the O 1s peaks as compared to BiOCl. Hence, the increase of OVs in the 3 %-RPC-BOC may be attributed to a little carbon doping that occurred during the one-pot solvothermal process. More adsorbed oxygen atoms on vacant sites may enhance photocatalytic activity by increasing the formation of reactive oxygen species, such as superoxide radicals.

The optical properties of all photocatalysts were evaluated using UV–vis DRS. Fig. 6a indicates that all photocatalysts have the ability to absorb both ultraviolet and visible light. The RPC-BOC photocatalysts show a higher light absorption performance than pure BiOCl. The addition of RPC resulted in a displacement of the absorption edge of BiOCl, transitioning from 367 nm in pure BiOCl to 382 nm in 3 %-RPC-BOC. Photocatalysts containing more than 1 wt% RPC improved light absorption intensity in the 370–700 nm range (inset of Fig. 6a), while pure BiOCl had the lowest light absorption within this range. The 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst shows the largest amount of absorption over the entire visible region, with a redshift in its maximum absorbance [14]. This observation suggests that the incorporation of RPC in the BiOCl photocatalyst changed its electronic structure, leading to a broader range of light absorption and an increase in light absorption capacity.

The energy band (Eg) of photocatalysts is shown in Fig. 6b. An Eg of 3.24 eV was found for the BiOCl photocatalyst, which is in line with the tetragonal crystalline-phase BiOCl [39]. This discovery provided further support for the XRD results. The modification of BiOCl with RPC resulted in a decrease in their Eg values. The minimum value observed in 3 %-RPC-BOC was 3.13 eV. The decrease in Eg was attributed to the plentiful OVs present on the crystal surface. Furthermore, the increase in these OVs generated a new energy level within the forbidden bandwidth range of 3 %-RPC-BOC [40]. The energy band between the valence band and the new energy level created by OVs (EOVs) can be calculated using Eq. (1) [5]:

which λ is the peak corresponding to the maximum absorption wavelength of the 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst (570 nm). The Eg is 2.17 eV. Thus, the potential position of the EOVs is 0.5 eV.

The valence band binding energy (EVB) of the 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst was examined using the XPS-VB analysis, as shown in Fig. 6c. It was measured at 2.67 eV. The energy band structure equation was used to calculate the conduction band energy (ECB) of the 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst [11]. The definition of the equation is provided in Eq. (2):
(2) ECB = EVB – Eg

where Eg denotes the value of 3.13 eV (see Fig. 6b). Thus, the ECB of 3 %-RPC-BOC is −0.46 eV. The pure BiOCl band structure, on the other hand, had an Eg of 3.24 eV, an EVB of 3.47 eV [41], and an ECB of 0.23 eV. The valence band and conduction band positions of the 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst were altered. The presence of oxygen vacancies not only affected the bandgap between the valence and conduction bands but also modified their relative positions.

In order to verify the improvement in the separation and transfer of photogenerated charges after the addition of RPC, the surface photovoltage, photocurrent, and electrochemical impedance of the photocatalysts were analyzed. Fig. 7a displays the SPS results. A robust response in the 300–375 nm and 527–618 nm ranges. All RPC-BOC photocatalysts present a more pronounced SPS signal than BiOCl within the 300–375 nm range, with 3 %-RPC-BOC exhibiting the highest intensity. Fig. 7b depicts the SPS signals for BiOCl and RPC-BOC composites within the wavelength range of 525–610 nm. This range of SPS signals provides insight into the photocatalysts' visible light absorption and photoinduced charge separation efficiency. The 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst clearly had the strongest SPS signal in this wavelength range. It is followed by the 2 %-RPC-BOC, then the 1 %-RPC-BOC, and finally the 4 %-RPC-BOC. BiOCl had the weakest signal. The higher SPS signal of RPC-BOC composites indicates that they are better at absorbing visible light and separating photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The optimal incorporation of RPC was responsible for the higher SPS signal in the 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst. The creation of OVs in the BiOCl matrix increased the number of electrons and made it easier for the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, which slowed down the rate at which photogenerated charges recombined [42]. Several studies have reported that surface photovoltage production in the visible light range is a result of electron capture by oxygen vacancies [5,19,26]. Fig. 7c presents the PC results for all photocatalysts. The 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst exhibits the strongest PC signal, while pure BiOCl displays the lowest. A stronger PC signal indicates that more electrons and holes are successfully separated and involved in the photocatalytic activity. This is further supported by the EIS measurements, as shown in Fig. 7d. A decrease in the arc radius in the EIS spectrum was associated with a reduction in impedance and an increase in carrier separation efficiency [43]. The EIS signal of 3 %-RPC-BOC was lower than that of BiOCl, indicating better charge separation. The results of SPS, PC, and ESI all confirm that the 3 %-RPC-BOC had the highest efficiency for photogenerated charge separation. In comparison, the 4 %-RPC-BOC shows lower SPS, EIS, and PC signals. This reduction is due to the changes in OVs caused by the variations in RPC quantities added. Excessive concentrations of OVs can lead to photogenerated charge recombination, thereby reducing the effective separation of charges and overall photocatalytic performance [33]. Thus, higher SPS, PC, and ESI signals directly correlate with enhanced photocatalytic efficiency by ensuring better charge separation and utilization in the photocatalytic process.

The enhancement of OVs in the photocatalyst was also confirmed by low-temperature ESR, as depicted in Fig. 8. The ESR signal at 3360 G for 3 %-RPC-BOC was much stronger than that for BiOCl. This suggests that the addition of 3 % wt% RPC during BiOCl synthesis can alter the amount of OVs in the photocatalyst. RPC containing carbon can easily attach to the oxygen atoms in the Bi-O bond, causing the Bi-O lattice bond to break down, resulting in OVs. It can be concluded that the incorporation of RPC into the BiOCl photocatalyst allows us to adjust the number of OVs. Moreover, it is expected that an increase in OVs can enhance the production of superoxide radicals and the separation of photogenerated charges during the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants, which would make the process more effective [39,44].

The efficiency of the prepared photocatalysts was evaluated through the photocatalytic degradation of RhB. Initially, the photocatalysts were allowed to reach an equilibrium state of RhB adsorption-desorption during a 30-min dark reaction. Subsequently, the photocatalytic degradation was carried out under light exposure. The degradation efficiencies of the photocatalysts were recorded, as shown in Fig. 9a. After 48 min of light exposure, the BiOCl photocatalyst achieved a degradation efficiency of 50.4 % for RhB. The incorporation of RPC significantly enhanced the photocatalytic performance. The 3 %-RPC-BOC had the highest efficiency in degrading RhB, achieving a removal of 98 % in 48 min under visible light. The SPS, PC, and EIS results all agreed with these results, indicating that the 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst outperformed the others. The optimal concentration of OVs in the 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst may promote better charge separation and transfer.

The first-order rate constant, given in Fig. 9b, exhibits a uniform trend across all photocatalysts. The 3 % RPC-BOC had the highest first-order rate constant of 0.071 min−1. The decomposition of the organic compounds produced by the RhB degradation into smaller molecules of carbon dioxide and water was also investigated, as shown in Fig. 9c. The 3 %-RPC-BOC shows a remarkable 45 % efficiency in eliminating total organic carbon from the solution. Fig. 9d illustrates the RhB degradation process using the 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst. The absorbance of the RhB solution was measured at different time intervals to monitor the degradation. As the reaction time increased, the absorption peak moved towards shorter wavelengths, indicating the gradual conversion of RhB into smaller organic molecules before its ultimate degradation into carbon dioxide and water during the degradation process [45].

Tetracycline (TC) degradation tests were also conducted to evaluate the efficacy of photocatalysts in decomposing various organic compounds. Fig. 9e shows that both 3 %-RPC-BOC and BiOCl had the capability to degrade TC. Particularly, 3 %-RPC-BOC achieved a TC removal rate of 82.1 % after 180 min, indicating higher degradation performance compared to pure BiOCl. During the blank test, the organic carbon content in the TC solution did not change significantly. In Fig. 9f, the degradation rate of 3 %-RPC-BOC was 2.21 times higher than that of pure BiOCl. The 3 % RPC-BOC photocatalyst demonstrates excellent removal rates for both RhB and TC. These compounds may be entirely eliminated if they are exposed to adequate light for an extended period of time.

The stability of a photocatalyst is required for its practical application in wastewater treatment, a photocatalyst must be stable. The 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst was subjected to cycling experiments in order to evaluate its reusability and stability. The results are shown in Figs. 10a and 10b. The crystal structure of the photocatalyst remained unchanged even after performing seven operational cycles, as shown in Fig. 10a. Despite a little decline in the RhB removal effectiveness of the 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst, the overall degradation efficiency remained consistently high throughout the 7 cycles of testing (Fig. 10b). The 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst indicates excellent stability in degrading RhB, thereby rendering it suitable for recycling and enhancing its potential for practical use.

Table 1 summarizes the comparative performance of various photocatalysts in the degradation of RhB. Among all the photocatalysts shown in Table 1, the 3 %-RPC-BiOCl with a flower-like structure exhibited the highest RhB removal efficiency of 98.0 % in only 48 min when exposed to a Xe lamp. This high degradation performance was achieved by using an inadequate catalyst dosage of only 1.0 g/L. The high efficiency of the 3 %-RPC-BiOCl, along with its low catalyst quantity, short reaction time, and high stability, suggests its good performance and potential for practical applications in wastewater treatment. The excellent photoactivity of the 3 %-RPC-BiOCl photocatalyst in degrading RhB strongly supports the efficacy of in-situ synthesis of BiOCl incorporated with rosemary powder as an innovative method.

The degrading performance of photocatalysts is related to the active radicals [51]. ESR analysis was conducted on BiOCl and 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalysts to examine the variations in active radicals [11], as shown in Fig. 11a and b. The hydroxyl (·OH) and superoxide (·O2 -) radicals were detected in both BiOCl and 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalysts. The 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst had stronger signals of ·OH and ·O2 - compared to BiOCl. This is likely because adding RSM decreased the complexation of hole (h+) and electron (e-), which led to more radicals in the photocatalysts. Radical trapping studies [52,53] were conducted to determine the primary radical involved in photocatalysis. The results are presented in Fig. 11c. The scavengers used in this study were tert-butanol (TBA) to capture ·OH, tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) to trap h+, and benzoquinone (BQ) to trap ·O2 - [19,54]. The presence of benzoquinone resulted in the least effective degradation of RhB, suggesting that ·O2 - is the primary active radical in the photocatalytic degradation, whereas h+ and ·OH are secondary active radicals. The h+ radical exerted a more significant influence compared to ·OH. It is important to note that the overall photocatalytic degradation efficiency was influenced by the combined action of multiple reactive species, with ·O2 - playing the most significant role, followed by h+, and then ·OH. The concentration of ·O2 - in the photocatalyst was assessed using the nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) test, as presented in Fig. 11d. NBT can cause a chemical reaction with ·O2 - when exposed to light. The amount of residual NBT decreases as the concentration of ·O2 - in the system increases [5,55]. The solution with the added photocatalyst had a significantly lower absorbance at 259 nm than the blank solution. This suggests that the photocatalyst in the solution generated ·O2 - radicals when exposed to light. The absorbance of 3 %-RPC-BOC was lower compared to BiOCl, indicating a higher production of ·O2 - radicals by 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalysts. This is in line with the ESR analysis.

The photocatalytic degradation mechanism of the 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst is proposed in Fig. 12. This mechanism was obtained after a comprehensive analysis of all the experimental results. Typically, BiOCl is activated and generates electron-hole pairs when it is illuminated. The electrons produced are transferred from the valence band (VB), which comprises O 2p and Cl 2p orbitals, to the conduction band (CB), which comprises Bi 6p orbitals. This process results in the formation of holes in the valence band [56–58]. Nevertheless, the complexity of these pathways and the rapid charge recombination limit the degradation efficiency of BiOCl [59–61]. By incorporating an appropriate amount of RPC into BiOCl, the OVs are enhanced. Additionally, a novel energy level referred to as EOVs occurs with a magnitude of 0.5 eV. The EOVs is more positive than the ECB. Due to the presence of the OVs intermediate energy level, the photogenerated electrons can move from the valence band to the EOVs. At this level, OVs can directly capture some of these electrons, whereas others can be further excited and moved to the conduction band. This increases the effective path for electron transfer and enhances the efficiency of light utilization. In addition, when the excess electrons in the conduction band are unable to directly engage in the photocatalytic process, they may be trapped by OVs. The absence of oxygen atoms results in a positive charge on the OVs site. This positive charge helps enhance the presence of electrons in the surrounding area and decreases the formation of complexes between photogenerated electrons and holes [62,63]. The valence band of the 3 %-RPC-BOC has a higher potential (EVB = 2.67 eV) compared to the standard redox potential of E·OH/OH- (1.99 eV) and E·OH/H2O (2.37 eV) [14,64]. This means that the positive charge (h+) on the valence band may effectively convert OH- or H2O into a highly oxidizing ·OH radical and immediately react with the pollutant. The ·OH radical can break down the contaminant into smaller organic molecules as well as water and carbon dioxide. Furthermore, the conduction band potential of the 3 %-RPC-BOC (ECB = −0.46 eV) is lower than that of EO2/·O2- (-0.33 eV) [14,65]. Electrons in the conduction band can convert O2 to ·O2 -. Meanwhile, the CQDs in the 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst can break the Bi-O bond in the lattice of BiOCl by removing oxygen atoms. The unique delocalized conjugated structure of CQDs allows them to function as efficient electron acceptors [44,66]. This enables the easy transfer of photogenerated electrons to CQDs, which in turn promotes the creation of ·O2 -. Holes, ·O2 -, and ·OH radicals have strong oxidizing characteristics, which can break down RhB or TC molecules into water and carbon dioxide.

The main challenge with BiOCl as a photocatalyst is its rapid carrier recombination and limited visible light absorption, which restrict its efficiency. This study addresses these issues by incorporating rosemary powder into BiOCl via a facile solvothermal process. RPC induced a significant change in BiOCl, transforming its lamellar structure into a floral microstructure. This transformation resulted in an increase in both surface area and pore volume. The CQDs were discovered inside the microstructure of the 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst, which enhanced the photocatalytic activity. The 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst demonstrated significantly enhanced OVs, leading to improved charge separation, reduced recombination, and increased generation of active radicals. This photocatalyst achieved 98 % RhB degradation and 45 % organic carbon removal within 48 min under visible light, outperforming many state-of-the-art photocatalysts. It is capable of effectively breaking down a wide range of pollutants as well as being highly active in the degradation of TC. The 3 %-RPC-BOC exhibits good stability and reusability even after undergoing seven cycles. Consequently, the 3 %-RPC-BOC photocatalyst has significant potential for practical applications in wastewater treatment due to its superior efficiency and stability.
